answers.field_label: Notes On Nutrition Guidelines For Foods And Beverages At School
answers.value:
This section is being completed by Maria Hernandez, Wellness Committee Chair and Assistant Principal at Riverside Middle School, part of the Riverview Unified School District. I have overseen implementation of our updated Local School Wellness Policy since it was revised and approved by the school board on 2023-06-15.

Food Fundraisers:
Our district wellness policy limits the number of food-based fundraisers that do not fully meet Smart Snacks standards to align with PDE guidance. At Riverside Middle, each student organization is allowed up to 2 exempt food fundraisers per school year, and these may not occur during the breakfast or lunch service period. We maintain a master calendar of fundraisers in the main office, and all proposed fundraisers must be approved by the principal and reviewed by our food service director. Over the past year, we have shifted most fundraisers to nonfood options (such as walk-a-thons, read-a-thons, and school spirit wear) or to healthier food options like fruit cups, whole-grain pretzels, and bottled water. We have communicated the exemption rules to staff, students, and parent organizations via email, staff meetings, and our school website.

Standards for Foods and Beverages Offered for Free:
Our written wellness policy includes specific local standards for foods and beverages that are offered for free to students, such as classroom celebrations, reward activities, and school-wide events. These standards require that:
- At least 50% of foods offered at classroom celebrations must meet Smart Snacks standards (e.g., fresh fruit, vegetables with low-fat dip, whole-grain crackers, low-fat yogurt).
- Sugary beverages (including soda and energy drinks) may not be offered for free to students at any time during the school day or at school-sponsored events on campus.
- Teachers are strongly encouraged to use nonfood rewards (extra recess, classroom privileges, positive notes home) instead of candy or snacks.
- Holiday and birthday celebrations should be consolidated (e.g., once monthly) to reduce the number of days with treats, and families are encouraged to send nonfood items such as pencils, stickers, or small books instead of cupcakes or candy.
We shared these standards with families at the start of the school year through the student handbook and a detailed email from the principal, and we revisit them at back-to-school night.

Nonfood Ideas and Healthy Alternatives:
We developed and distributed a two-page list of nonfood celebration and reward ideas, as well as examples of healthier food and beverage options, in collaboration with our school nurse and district dietitian. Examples of nonfood ideas include: homework passes, classroom dance breaks, extra art or music time, board game time, and recognition certificates. Healthy food alternatives we promote include: sliced apples, orange wedges, baby carrots with hummus, popcorn that is air-popped and lightly salted, low-fat string cheese, and infused water (such as water with sliced cucumbers or lemons). This list is posted on our school website, included in staff substitute folders, and kept in the front office for parents who inquire about acceptable options.

Marketing and Promotion:
Our wellness policy states that only foods and beverages that meet USDA Smart Snacks standards may be marketed or advertised to students during the school day, including on vending machines, posters, scoreboards, and school publications. Over the last year, we removed several outdated posters and vending machine wraps that featured regular soda and candy. We replaced these with neutral or school-spirit themed designs and, in some cases, with promotional materials highlighting water, fruits, and vegetables. Our vending contracts were reviewed with the district business office to ensure that all items sold during the school day meet Smart Snacks criteria. We also asked our parent-teacher organization to avoid using branded high-sugar products in flyers or event promotions.

Current Challenges and Next Steps:
While we have made significant progress, implementation is still considered partially in place because we continue to address a few lingering issues:
- Some staff members, especially new hires, are still adjusting to the shift away from candy rewards. We are planning a short professional development session in early 2026 to provide more concrete classroom management strategies that do not rely on food.
- A small number of after-school clubs occasionally bring in outside snacks that do not meet Smart Snacks standards. Although after-school time is technically outside the defined school day, we are working to align these practices with the spirit of the policy and encourage healthier options.
- We are in the process of developing simple monitoring tools (brief checklists and quarterly walkthroughs) to ensure that marketing and fundraising practices continue to comply with both federal and local standards.

Overall, our school is moving steadily toward full implementation of the nutrition guidelines, with strong support from our principal, food service staff, and a growing number of teachers and parents. We plan to review and update our practices annually based on feedback from the Wellness Committee and student surveys.

answers.field_label: Report On The Progress Made In Attaining The Goals Of The Wellness Policy
answers.value:
This narrative is submitted by Maria Hernandez, Assistant Principal and Wellness Committee Chair at Riverside Middle School (Riverview Unified School District), for the 2024–2025 school year.

Overview of Wellness Policy Goals and Governance:
Our district wellness policy was revised and adopted by the school board on 2023-06-15 to align with current USDA requirements and PDE guidance. The policy addresses nutrition standards for all foods available on campus, nutrition promotion and education, physical activity, and other school-based activities that support student wellness. At Riverside Middle School (grades 6–8, approximately 640 students), implementation is overseen by a building-level Wellness Committee that includes the principal (Dr. Kevin Patel), myself (Maria Hernandez), our school nurse (Lauren Kim, RN), our food service manager (James O’Malley), two classroom teachers, one physical education teacher, a parent representative, and one student representative from the 8th grade.

Progress in Nutrition Standards for School Meals:
Our school participates in the National School Lunch Program and School Breakfast Program. Over the past two years, we have made the following progress:
- All reimbursable meals meet or exceed current USDA meal pattern requirements, including whole grain-rich products, age-appropriate calorie limits, and sodium targets.
- We increased the variety of fruits and vegetables offered by implementing a three-week rotating menu cycle. For example, students now regularly see options such as fresh spinach salads, roasted sweet potatoes, and seasonal fresh fruit (apples, pears, oranges, and melon cups).
- Our food service manager has conducted informal taste tests with students—such as sampling whole-grain pasta and new vegetable side dishes—and used their feedback to refine recipes. This has reduced plate waste for certain items that were initially unpopular.
- Participation in the breakfast program has increased from an average of 24% of students daily in 2022–2023 to approximately 31% in 2024–2025, which we attribute partly to improved menu appeal (e.g., breakfast burritos made with whole-grain tortillas, yogurt parfaits with fruit and granola) and better promotion during morning announcements.

Progress in Competitive Foods, Fundraisers, and Free Foods:
In line with our wellness policy and Smart Snacks standards, we have:
- Completed a full review of all snacks and beverages sold in the school store and vending machines. Items that did not meet Smart Snacks (such as regular chips and full-sugar sports drinks) were removed and replaced with compliant options like baked chips, low-sugar granola bars, and bottled water.
- Implemented a building-wide system to track food-related fundraisers. During the 2024–2025 school year, only 6 fundraisers involved food, and 4 of these used items that met Smart Snacks standards (e.g., trail mix, dried fruit, and whole-grain popcorn). The remaining 2 were approved as limited exemptions and scheduled outside of meal service times.
- Communicated clear expectations to families and staff regarding free foods at celebrations and events. Teachers report that many families have shifted from sending cupcakes and candy for birthdays to sending fruit trays, yogurt, or nonfood items such as pencils, erasers, or small notebooks. While occasional exceptions still occur, the overall trend is toward healthier options and fewer classroom disruptions due to high-sugar treats.

Nutrition Education and Promotion:
Our wellness policy calls for integrating nutrition education into the curriculum and promoting healthy eating habits across the school environment. Progress includes:
- Health classes in all three grade levels (6–8) now include at least two units per year on nutrition, label reading, and the relationship between food choices, energy levels, and long-term health. Teachers use MyPlate resources and interactive activities where students compare the nutritional content of different snacks.
- Our physical education teachers incorporate brief discussions about hydration and fueling the body for physical activity. For example, before the fitness testing unit, they review why water and balanced meals are preferable to sugary drinks and energy drinks.
- We launched a monthly “Wellness Wednesday” feature on the morning announcements. Students share short messages about topics such as the importance of eating breakfast, trying new vegetables, and staying hydrated. These messages are often written by the student representative on the Wellness Committee, which has helped increase peer engagement.
- Posters promoting fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and water are displayed in the cafeteria and main hallways. We removed all outdated or conflicting signage (such as posters advertising soda) to ensure consistent messaging.

Physical Activity and Physical Education:
Our wellness policy emphasizes regular physical activity opportunities beyond the minimum PE requirements. Progress at Riverside Middle includes:
- All students are scheduled for physical education at least two days per week, with many 6th graders receiving three days depending on their elective rotation. Classes focus on a mix of fitness, team sports, and lifelong physical activities such as walking, yoga, and basic strength exercises.
- Several teachers have adopted short “brain break” movement activities in their classrooms, especially in 6th grade. These are 3–5 minute stretching or movement sessions used between lessons to help students refocus. While not yet universal, this practice is spreading as teachers see positive effects on student behavior and attention.
- We started a before-school walking club that meets twice per week on the track from 7:30–7:50 a.m. Participation is voluntary and averages about 30–40 students per session, with some staff members joining. Students who attend regularly have reported feeling more awake and ready to learn.
- Recess is not formally scheduled at the middle school level, but we have opened the gym on certain days during lunch for structured activities supervised by staff volunteers. This is still in a pilot phase, but early feedback from students has been positive.

Staff, Family, and Community Involvement:
The wellness policy calls for ongoing involvement from staff and families in wellness initiatives. Our progress includes:
- The Wellness Committee meets at least three times per year (September, January, and April). Meeting agendas and brief summaries are shared with staff via email and posted on the school website.
- We included a short wellness survey in our fall parent communication, asking families about their priorities and concerns. Many parents expressed interest in more information about packing healthy lunches and quick, affordable dinners. In response, our nurse and food service manager are preparing a simple “Healthy Eating at Home” tip sheet to distribute later this spring.
- Staff wellness has become a small but growing focus. This year, we ran a voluntary step challenge for staff over six weeks in the fall. About 25 staff members participated, forming teams and tracking steps using personal devices. While informal, this effort helped model healthy behavior for students.

Monitoring, Evaluation, and Transparency:
To meet the requirement for periodic assessment and public reporting, we have:
- Conducted this self-assessment using the district’s Wellness Policy Assessment Tool, documenting which elements are fully, partially, or not yet in place at Riverside Middle.
- Collected basic data on school meal participation, sales of competitive foods, and the number and type of food-related fundraisers.
- Reviewed anecdotal feedback from teachers, students, and families regarding changes in snacks, celebrations, and reward practices.
- Posted a summary of our wellness policy and a brief report of this year’s progress on the Riverview Unified School District website, with a link from the Riverside Middle School page. We also plan to include a one-page summary in our end-of-year newsletter.

Remaining Challenges and Future Goals:
Although we have made meaningful progress, several areas still require attention:
- Consistency across classrooms: A small number of classrooms still rely on candy or sugary snacks as rewards, especially around holidays and testing periods. Our goal for next year is to provide targeted professional development and share more practical, nonfood reward strategies.
- After-school environment: While the policy primarily addresses the school day, some after-school clubs and athletic events continue to sell or serve high-sugar snacks and beverages. We aim to gradually align these practices with the spirit of the wellness policy by offering more balanced options and educating coaches and club advisors.
- Student voice: We have one student representative on the Wellness Committee, but we would like to expand student input through brief surveys and focus groups, particularly around menu planning and physical activity opportunities.
- Data collection: Our current monitoring is partly anecdotal. Over the next year, we plan to use simple checklists during cafeteria walkthroughs and maintain a centralized log of classroom celebrations and fundraisers involving food. This will help us track trends and identify areas where additional guidance is needed.

Conclusion:
Overall, Riverside Middle School has made steady and tangible progress toward attaining the goals outlined in the district wellness policy. School meals meet federal standards, competitive foods are largely compliant with Smart Snacks, and we have significantly reduced the reliance on high-sugar foods for celebrations and rewards. Nutrition education and physical activity opportunities have increased, and there is growing awareness among staff, students, and families about the importance of a healthy school environment.

While some aspects of implementation are still considered partially in place—particularly around classroom rewards, after-school events, and systematic data collection—we have clear plans and timelines to address these areas. The Wellness Committee will continue to meet regularly, review data, and adjust strategies to ensure that our wellness policy remains a living document that genuinely supports the health and academic success of all Riverside Middle School students.
